useCases: Technical calculations for healthy snack planning
explanation:
**Nutrition and fitness** rely on physical principles (energy balance) and biological factors (macronutrients, micronutrients). This calculator uses formulas validated by the scientific community (ACSM, ISSN, NIH).

## How It's Calculated

Healthy 150-300 calorie snacks.

This calculator applies the formula with your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to unusual values.

## Standard Caloric Distribution

| Macronutrient | % Calories | kcal/gram |
|---|---|---|
| Protein | 15-35% | 4 kcal/g |
| Carbohydrates | 45-65% | 4 kcal/g |
| Fats | 20-35% | 9 kcal/g |
| Alcohol | — | 7 kcal/g |
| Fiber | Minimum 25-38g/day | — |

## When to Consult a Nutritionist

- If you have health conditions (diabetes, hypertension, kidney disease) requiring personalized nutrition.
- If you want significant muscle gain or plan to compete in sports.
- If you have eating disorders (binge eating, extreme restriction).
- If you haven't made progress toward your goal for 3+ months.
- To plan complete vegetarian or vegan nutrition.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult with a healthcare professional. All values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How many calories should a healthy snack have?
faq.a: A healthy snack typically ranges from 150-300 calories. This amount provides sustained energy without disrupting meals.

faq.q: What are good options for 150-300 calorie snacks?
faq.a: Protein-rich options: yogurt with berries, nuts, cheese. Balanced options: apple with peanut butter, granola bar, hummus and veggies.

faq.q: Is snacking bad for weight loss?
faq.a: Strategic snacking can support weight loss by preventing overeating at meals. Choose nutrient-dense snacks and watch portions.
Yes. Guidelines follow current 2026 research from ACSM, ISSN, and NIH.

faq.q: Should I snack if I'm trying to lose weight?
faq.a: Yes, but make them count. Healthy snacks maintain metabolism and prevent overeating. Total daily calories still matter most.

faq.q: How much protein should I eat daily?
faq.a: Sedentary: 0.8g per kg. Active: 1.2-1.5g per kg. Muscle building: 1.6-2.2g per kg body weight.

faq.q: When should I eat a snack?
faq.a: Best times: mid-morning, afternoon, or pre/post-workout. Avoid snacking within 2 hours of main meals.

faq.q: Should I talk to my doctor before changing my diet?
faq.a: Yes, especially if you have health conditions, take medications, or plan major dietary changes.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
